metadata.summary: Technique for expressing functional requirements as actor-system interaction sequences with main, alternative, and exception flows that map directly to testable scenarios.

content.title: Use Case Modeling
content.summary: A technique for expressing functional requirements as actor-system interaction sequences. Each use case captures WHO achieves WHAT goal with the system and HOW the system responds across normal, alternative, and exception flows. Use case names follow Verb+Noun convention; each has preconditions, a main flow (5-9 steps), alternative flows, exception flows, and postconditions.

why.p: Requirements written as feature lists omit actor context, miss edge cases, and produce untestable specs. Use cases force the BA to name a concrete actor and trace every path — including failures — before development starts. Each flow maps directly to a test path, closing the gap between "specified" and "testable".

list.item: Functional requirements for transactional systems (line-of-business apps, regulated software) before development starts
Multiple actor types interact with the system and cross-actor collisions have already caused bugs
Compliance/audit context (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, SOX) requiring actor-goal → flow → code → test traceability
Legacy system migration where screens are reverse-engineered into a controlled spec
Test engineers need a deterministic scenario source for end-to-end test design
Pre-development alignment when stakeholders speak features and developers need flows
Pure discovery phase — Opportunity Solution Trees give better signal than premature formalization
Agile teams operating well with user stories + acceptance criteria — UC specs duplicate work
Pure data/analytics platforms where data flow models fit better than actor-goal sequences
ML/LLM features with probabilistic responses — use cases assume deterministic system behavior
Tiny CRUD apps with one actor and fewer than 10 screens — an acceptance criteria sheet is faster
Event-driven systems where event topology matters more than actor goals; use event storming or BPMN

section.name: use-case-components

rule.statement: Every use case must identify: actor (who), goal (what), system boundary (what is in scope), and scenarios (how).
rule.rationale: Missing any component produces an incomplete spec that cannot drive test case generation or traceability.

section.p: Actor types: primary actors initiate use cases to achieve their own goals; secondary actors support the system (external services, databases); system actors are automated timers or other systems.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Identify actors: list all human roles, external systems, and timers that interact with the system boundary.
rule.rationale: Actors missed at this stage produce use cases with no owner and no test persona.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Identify use cases: for each actor ask what tasks they perform, what information they need, what they create or change, and what events trigger their actions. Name each use case Verb+Noun.
rule.rationale: Verb+Noun naming enforces goal orientation and prevents feature-as-use-case mistakes.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Create use case diagram: visualize actors outside the system boundary, use cases inside. Show include/extend/generalize relationships only when they de-duplicate 10 or more steps.
rule.rationale: Premature relationship modeling is the #1 source of UC diagram errors; relationships should emerge, not be designed in advance.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Write use case specifications: document name, ID, description, actors, preconditions, main flow (5-9 steps), alternative flows, exception flows, postconditions, and business rules for each use case.
rule.rationale: Specifications without exception flows miss 40%+ of real behavior and leave testing without negative-path guidance.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Validate with stakeholders: confirm all actor goals are covered, main flow is correct, alternatives are complete, exceptions are handled, and postconditions are observable.
rule.rationale: Validation is cheapest before development; each missed exception discovered in testing costs 10x more to fix.

rule.statement: Use Include only when the included use case is always executed as part of the including use case and de-duplicates 10 or more steps across multiple use cases.
rule.rationale: Include is misused ~80% of the time; most apparent includes are just consecutive steps within a single flow.

rule.statement: Use Extend only when an optionally added behavior branches from a specific extension point in the base use case.
rule.rationale: Extend adds complexity; if the extension is not truly optional and conditional, model it as an alternative flow instead.

rule.statement: Use Generalization when a specialized actor or use case inherits all behavior of a parent and adds or overrides specific steps.
rule.rationale: Generalization is the least-used relationship; apply only when the IS-A semantic is genuinely true.

rule.statement: Use case names must be Verb+Noun (e.g. Place Order, Generate Report). Reject any name that is a noun only, a screen name, or contains UI elements.
rule.rationale: Noun-only names describe system state, not actor goals; UI names couple the spec to a design that will change.

rule.statement: Main flow must have 5-9 steps. Fewer than 5 indicates a system function that belongs inside another use case; more than 9 indicates a use case that should be split.
rule.rationale: Step-count uniformity enables comparison and traceability; unbounded length makes review and testing unmanageable.

rule.statement: One step = one actor action plus one system response. Apply this heuristic uniformly across all use cases in a project.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent granularity makes cross-UC comparison and test-case generation unreliable.

rule.statement: Every use case must have at least one alternative flow and at least one exception flow before it is accepted as complete.
rule.rationale: Exception flows are the highest-value section; humans and agents skip them by default, leaving 40%+ of real behavior unspecified.

rule.statement: Postconditions must be observable system states (database record created, message sent, file written) — not user perceptions.
rule.rationale: Unobservable postconditions cannot be verified by automated tests or acceptance criteria.

rule.statement: System Response column must contain system-behavior verbs (validates, persists, emits, calculates) — never UI-rendering verbs (renders, shows, displays button).
rule.rationale: UI verbs couple the spec to a specific design; system-behavior verbs remain stable across redesigns.

checklist.check: Each use case has a clear, named primary actor
At least one alternative flow documented
At least one exception flow documented
Business rules are referenced, not embedded
Reviewed and signed off by stakeholders

antipattern.description: LLMs write the happy path and stop, omitting alternative and exception flows.
antipattern.reason: Force at least one alternative and one exception in the output schema; reject any output that omits them.

antipattern.description: Drafter agents insert UI details ("displays popup") even after being told not to.
antipattern.reason: Add a post-generation regex check for UI nouns (button, modal, popup, screen, click) and bounce matching drafts.

antipattern.description: Agents hallucinate actors that are UI elements (Header, Sidebar) rather than human or system roles.
antipattern.reason: Constrain actor type to {human, system, timer} in the output schema and reject anything else.

antipattern.description: Drafting 10+ use cases in a single LLM call — accuracy collapses due to template repetition errors.
antipattern.reason: Chunk one UC per call; combine only at the index-generation step.

example.title: UC-101: Place Order
example.code:
Main Flow: | Step | Actor Action | System Response | |------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------| | 1 | Customer initiates checkout | System displays order summary with totals | | 2 | Customer confirms shipping address | System calculates shipping cost | | 3 | Customer selects payment method | System presents payment entry form | | 4 | Customer submits payment details | System validates payment with payment gateway | | 5 | Customer confirms order | System creates order, reduces inventory, | | | | emits confirmation email |
Alternative Flow AF-1: New Shipping Address At step 2, customer enters a new address. System validates format and saves address to customer profile. Return to step 2 with updated address selected.
Exception Flow EX-1: Payment Declined At step 4, payment gateway returns decline response. System displays error "Payment could not be processed" with error code. Customer may retry with same or different payment method; flow resumes at step 3.
Postconditions (success): Order record created with status Confirmed; inventory decremented for each line item; confirmation email delivered. Postconditions (failure): Cart preserved; no inventory change; no charge.
example.note: Primary Actor: Customer. Preconditions: customer is logged in; cart has at least one item. Note: postconditions are observable states, not user perceptions.

example.title: Actor-Goal Mapping Example
example.code:
| Actor | Use Case | Type | |-----------------|------------------------|---------| | Customer | Browse Products | Primary | | Customer | Place Order | Primary | | Customer | Track Shipment | Primary | | Admin | Manage Inventory | Primary | | Payment Gateway | Process Payment | System | | Warehouse System| Reserve Inventory | System |
Relationship: Place Order <<includes>> Process Payment Rationale: every order must process payment; de-duplicates the 4-step payment validation flow that would otherwise appear in Place Order, Renew Subscription, and Top-Up Account.
example.note: E-commerce system — actor-to-use-case index used to verify complete coverage.

antipattern.description: Feature as use case: "Display order screen" — no actor goal, no system behavior.
antipattern.reason: A use case must describe a goal an actor achieves, not a screen the system renders. Rename to "View Order Details" and add actor, flows, postconditions.

antipattern.description: Overly abstract use case: "Use the system" — no specific goal, untestable.
antipattern.reason: Abstract use cases cannot produce test cases; every use case must deliver a specific, measurable outcome for a named actor.

antipattern.description: Main flow with 20 steps — two distinct actor goals bundled in one use case.
antipattern.reason: Split at the natural goal boundary. Apply the step-count rule: any main flow exceeding 9 steps is a splitting signal.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: Use case bloat: teams generate UC-001..UC-200 documents that nobody re-reads. Useful UCs cap at approximately 30 per release; beyond that, hierarchy collapses.
LLMs over-include UI details ("user clicks blue button") which couple the spec to a specific design — agent prompts must explicitly forbid UI verbs and require system-behavior verbs.
Include and extend are misused approximately 80% of the time in human-authored UCs; LLMs amplify the confusion. Prefer flat UCs unless the include/extend genuinely de-duplicates 10+ steps.
Step granularity drifts across calls. Without a fixed step-counting heuristic, LLM output is even more uneven.
Exception flows are the highest-value section and the one humans skip; LLMs default to "Happy path looks great, please review" unless explicitly forced to enumerate failure modes.
Use cases assume a closed system boundary. Microservices / mesh architectures with shifting boundaries make "the system" ambiguous and UCs misleading.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive use case modeling as a four-stage pipeline (extract actors → enumerate goals → draft specs → validate completeness), each stage a discrete subagent invocation with structured I/O. Use Haiku for template-filling stages (actor extraction, main-flow drafting), Sonnet for review/validation/exception generation, Opus only for cross-UC relationship modeling (include, generalize). Persist artifacts as markdown UC specs under .aidocs/feature/use-cases/UC-NNN-slug.md with YAML front-matter for indexing, and emit a use-case-index.md at the feature level. The BA / PM stays human-in-the-loop on actor identification (cheapest place to fix mistakes) and on relationship modeling (highest cost of getting wrong).

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent once UC specs stabilize, hand off as input to spec/design generation in .aidocs/backlog/feature/. UC-NNN IDs become traceability anchors in spec.md and test-plan.md. Custom actor-extractor-agent (Haiku) input: project brief / existing screens / API surface. Output: JSON list of actors with type (primary/secondary/system) and one-line goal each. Custom uc-drafter-agent (Haiku) input: one actor + one goal + system context. Output: UC spec markdown filling the template. Forbid UI verbs in system prompt. Custom uc-reviewer-agent (Sonnet) input: a draft UC. Output: gap report keyed to the 9-item quality checklist. Required to flag missing exception flows. Custom uc-relationship-agent (Opus) input: full UC set. Output: include / extend / generalization graph with rationale per edge. Run last.
rule.rationale: Specialized agents with different models reduce rework and improve quality across the pipeline.

list.item: Pin the system boundary in the system prompt before every drafting call. "The System" must resolve to a concrete service / app / API surface, not a vague "the platform".
Cap main flow at 5-9 steps. Fewer than 5 indicates likely a system function, not a UC. More than 9 indicates should split or delegate via include.

Forbid UI nouns in System Response column ("renders modal", "shows blue button"). Replace with system-behavior verbs ("validates", "persists", "emits event"). Enforce in the LLM prompt as a hard rule.
Always require at least one alternative flow and one exception flow before accepting a UC as "complete". Agents skip these by default.
Use the same step-counting heuristic across all UCs (one actor action + one system response = one step). Without it, comparison and traceability collapse.
Store UCs as markdown with YAML front-matter (id, name, primary_actor, status, version). The front-matter feeds index/traceability scripts and CI checks.
Treat the UC index as the source of truth for test scaffolding: each UC main flow yields 1 happy-path test, each AF/EX yields 1 negative-path test. Generate test stubs from the index.
Validate UC names with a regex: verb + noun only. CI fails the PR if a UC violates the convention.
Re-run the reviewer agent on the full UC set whenever a new UC lands; relationship inference (include) is fragile and needs the global view.
Keep UI mockups out of UC files. Link from front-matter (mockup_url:) instead. The UC outlives the design system; the design system does not outlive the UC.

antipattern.description: LLMs default to writing the happy path and stopping.
antipattern.reason: Always force ≥1 alternative + ≥1 exception flow in the schema; reject outputs that omit them.

antipattern.description: Drafter agents invent UI details ("displays popup confirmation") even when told not to.
antipattern.reason: Add a post-generation regex check (button|modal|popup|screen|click) and bounce drafts that match.
Pin the heuristic in the system prompt ("one step = one actor action + one system response") AND validate step count post-hoc.

antipattern.description: Relationship inference (include, extend, generalization) is the highest-error part.
antipattern.reason: Run only Opus, only after all UCs are drafted, and require rationale per edge.

antipattern.description: LLMs hallucinate actors that are really UI elements ("Header", "Sidebar").
antipattern.reason: Force actor type in {human, system, time} in the JSON schema; reject anything else.

antipattern.description: Long context tempts "draft 30 UCs in one call".
antipattern.reason: Accuracy collapses past approximately 5 UCs per call due to template repetition errors. Chunk one UC per call.

antipattern.description: Agents conflate primary and secondary actors.
antipattern.reason: Make actor_type required and validate that exactly one primary actor is set per UC.

antipattern.description: Reviewer agents grade their own drafts too generously.
antipattern.reason: Use a different model family (or at minimum a different system prompt with adversarial framing) for review than for drafting.

antipattern.description: "Generate test cases from this UC" produces redundant tests when include is used.
antipattern.reason: Resolve includes inline before test generation, or de-duplicate by step ID.

template.purpose: Full use case specification shell (ID, actors, preconditions, main/alt/exception flows, postconditions, business rules)
Diagram notation template listing actors, use cases, and relationships
LLM prompt for drafting one UC with hard rules (verb+noun, 5-9 steps, no UI nouns, required exception flows)
LLM prompt for scoring a UC against quality checklist, output JSON
